Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.
student_answer: Using PLA for a flexible spatula is not recommended due to its low Tg. A polymer with a higher Tg should be chosen for this application to maintain its shape and flexibility at higher temperatures.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.
student_answer:
No, using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula may not be a good idea since its glass transition temperature (Tg) is relatively low (60-65°C). The Tg of a polymer determines its behavior at different temperatures. Below the Tg, the polymer is in a glassy or brittle state, while above the Tg, the polymer is in a rubbery or viscous state.

In the case of PLA, its low Tg indicates that it will remain in a rubbery or viscous state at room temperature, which means that it will not be as rigid or stiff as other materials with a higher Tg. This property may make the spatula too flexible or bendable, which would make it difficult to use effectively when frying eggs or other foods.

Therefore, when choosing a polymer for a flexible spatula, a material with a higher Tg, such as polyamide (nylon), would be a better choice. Polyamide has a Tg of around 90°C, which means it will be in a more solid-like state at room temperature and maintain its shape when used as a spatula. Polyamide is also known for its toughness, durability, and resistance to wear, making it a suitable material for applications such as kitchen utensils.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?

Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
student_answer: At rest, the molecules within the polymers are held in place by both the primary and secondary bonds between the molecules. When a load is applied, those bonds end up taking a large amount of stress, causing a large amount of bond breakage. As the bonds between molecules break, the more the molecules are allowed to stretch and align in the direction the load direction. The more the molecules stretch the less stress is placed on the intermolecular bonds until the stretched molecules and the bonds are able to withstand the load/breakage. In short, load breaks the intermolecular bonds at once, forcing the molecules to stretch to make up for it. Since that process is not instantaneous, it takes a little time for that stress redistribution to happen and thus the load change is greater at the beginning.

Question: The glass transition temperature (Tg) is the temperature for which a material goes from a viscous or rubbery state to a more solid-like state. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a polymer commonly used in 3D printing applications. It has a relatively low Tg of 60-65 °C. Knowing this, would using PLA for applications such as a flexible spatula (useful when frying eggs and such) be a good idea? Why?  If not, what property should be desired when choosing another polymer?
Expected Answer: No, PLA would not do well in this application. Because PLA’s Tg is around 65 C and the face of a frying pan can reach temps much higher than that, it is likely that the shape of the spatula would deform due to a transition to a viscous type of behavior. This can also be seen in the forming process of PLA in which the material is heated (to around 180 C) and then layered up using a 3D printer. Silicone is a much better material for this application with a Tg of -125 C (not +125 C!).The property desirable is high melting temperature and very low Tg. It should be rubbery at room temperature and yet melt at significantly high temperature (at least greater than 250-300 C). If PLA is used, it will undergo plastic deformation when exposed to the pan and will retain the deformed shape when cooled to room temperature. Silicone has Tg of -125 C and melting point of ~1400 C which makes it a good candidate for flexible spatula to be used for cooking.
student_answer: PLA should not be used to manufacture a flexible spatula. This is because PLA would not be able to withstand repeated twisting and bending as it will not be flexible enough, and its Tg value is too small because at high temperatures PLA will be too viscous. A better polymer would be one with a higher Tg temperature and more flexible properties.

Question: During the stress relaxation: polymers remain under a fixed strain. Using the definition of the Relaxation Time constant; "a material property that defines the time needed for the instantaneous stress to decrease to 37% of the initial stress", explain why the change in the load is greater at the beginning of the two minutes?
Expected Answer:
Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself.
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by:
$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-\lambda t)$,\nwhere, σ₀ is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant.
The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
student_answer: When a polymer is put under tensile stress, the polymer chains untangle and the material elongates. However, it is harder to untangle the chains initially. Over time, it is easier to stretch the material than it was originally. This is why we see a greater load at the beginning.

standard_answer: Relaxation follows an exponential decay. As the material relaxes, the rate of decay is proportional to the load itself. 
The polymers tested in this experiment exhibit a viscoelastic behavior, i.e., time-dependent material behavior. The equation for determining the instantaneous stress as a function of time (t), under constant strain, is given by \sigma(t)=\sigma_0 exp(-\lambda t) where \sigma_0 is the original stress and λ is a material constant. Thus, we have an exponentially decaying stress behavior, considering a relaxation time constant. The change in load is large at the beginning, then exponentially decays to a more constant value. This is because, under constant strain, the molecule chains of the polymers begin to rapidly untangle and the specimen elongates, thus becoming more ductile and decreasing the overall stress. As the chains become less and less untangled, the specimens lose their ductility. Thus, the change in loading becomes lesser than at the beginning. The relaxation time constant is a way to determine how fast this specimen reaches a certain point, defined as 37% of the initial stress. This serves as a method to compare the speed at which various materials stress-relax.
